---
title: "The Memory Wall"
date: 2025-07-04
sidebar: true
---


```{=html}
<script>document.body.classList.add('book-page');</script>
```

There is an old observation in computer architecture that has survived nearly every technological
revolution of the past half century.

Computers rarely become slower because they forget how to compute.


They become slower because they forget where they left their data.


The distinction appears almost trivial until one realizes how profoundly it has shaped modern
computing. Every major leap in processor performance has eventually encountered the same invisible
adversary. Arithmetic units become faster. Pipelines become deeper. Parallelism increases. Yet memory,
governed by entirely different physical constraints, improves far more slowly. The processor spends an
increasing fraction of its existence doing something profoundly undignified: waiting.


Computer architects gave this phenomenon a name decades ago.


The Memory Wall .


The phrase refers to the widening disparity between computational throughput and memory
performance. As processors accelerated, the time required to retrieve data failed to keep pace.
Eventually, computation itself ceased to be the dominant expense. The cost lay in moving information
to the place where computation could occur.


History has repeated this lesson with remarkable consistency.


Supercomputers discovered it.


Database systems discovered it.


Scientific simulations discovered it.


And, somewhat belatedly, large language models discovered it as well.


At first glance, the transformer appears almost immune to such concerns. Its computational profile is
dominated by dense matrix multiplications, precisely the sort of workload modern GPUs were designed
to accelerate. NVIDIA's Tensor Cores execute these operations at extraordinary throughput, often
performing hundreds of trillions of floating-point operations each second. Looking only at theoretical
compute capability, one might conclude that modern accelerators possess computational headroom to
spare.


Yet GPUs, like all processors, cannot multiply numbers that have not yet arrived.


To appreciate the significance of this observation, it is worth considering what occurs during
autoregressive inference. A language model does not generate an entire response simultaneously. It
produces one token, then another, then another, each prediction conditioned upon everything that
preceded it. Recomputing the entire attention mechanism for every previously generated token would
be computationally absurd. Instead, transformers retain intermediate representations—keys and values
—from earlier tokens within what has become known as the KV cache . Future attention operations
simply reuse these stored representations.


Conceptually, the optimization is elegant.


Physically, it is expensive.


Every additional token extends the cache. Every subsequent prediction must retrieve progressively
larger collections of keys and values before attention can be computed. Context windows that once
contained a few thousand tokens have expanded to hundreds of thousands and, increasingly, beyond
one million. The arithmetic required for attention has certainly grown, but another cost has expanded
far more rapidly: memory traffic.


The crucial point is that the KV cache is not merely large.


It is repeatedly accessed.


Unlike model parameters, which remain comparatively static during inference, the KV cache grows
continuously and must be revisited for every generated token. Each attention operation therefore
initiates an intricate choreography through the GPU memory hierarchy. Data residing within High
Bandwidth Memory must be transported across memory controllers into on-chip SRAM, distributed
among streaming multiprocessors, loaded into registers, and only then can tensor cores begin
performing the computations they were built to execute.


The computation itself may require only microseconds.


The journey often requires considerably longer.


This inversion surprises many engineers because discussions surrounding transformer efficiency
frequently emphasize computational complexity while neglecting arithmetic intensity. Complexity
describes how many operations an algorithm performs. Arithmetic intensity asks a different question:
how much useful computation is obtained for every byte moved through memory? High-performance
computing has long recognized that algorithms with poor arithmetic intensity frequently become
bandwidth-bound regardless of their theoretical computational complexity.


Modern transformers increasingly belong to this category.


The remarkable success of FlashAttention illustrates this point beautifully.


FlashAttention did not fundamentally alter the mathematics of self-attention. Instead, it reorganized the
computation so that intermediate results remained within fast on-chip memory whenever possible,
dramatically reducing expensive transfers to and from HBM. The achievement was architectural rather
than statistical. It acknowledged a reality that had become increasingly difficult to ignore: reducing
memory movement frequently produces larger practical gains than reducing arithmetic.


Yet FlashAttention solved only part of the problem.


It optimized how attention was computed.


It did not substantially reduce what needed to be stored.


As context windows continued their relentless expansion, another question gradually became
unavoidable.

Must every key and every value exist in its original dimensionality?


The question appears deceptively innocent.


Its implications are profound.


Machine learning has repeatedly demonstrated that high-dimensional representations frequently
possess surprisingly low intrinsic dimensionality. Principal Component Analysis, autoencoders, latent
diffusion models and countless other techniques exploit the observation that complex information
often resides upon comparatively compact manifolds. The explicit representation appears enormous.
The underlying information content frequently is not.


DeepSeek's Multi-head Latent Attention extends precisely this intuition into the transformer itself.


Rather than storing full key and value tensors for every token, MLA first projects these representations
into a substantially lower-dimensional latent space. During inference, the latent representation is
retained instead of the original tensors. When attention is later required, keys and values are
reconstructed from the compressed latent representation before computation proceeds.


At first encounter, the idea almost appears counterintuitive.


Compression is usually associated with loss.


Inference demands precision.


How can both simultaneously be true?


The answer lies in recognizing that the purpose of the KV cache is not faithful reconstruction in the
traditional signal-processing sense. The cache exists only to preserve the information required for
future attention computations. If the latent representation retains precisely those features that
attention ultimately depends upon, reconstructing the original tensors exactly becomes unnecessary.
The objective is functional equivalence rather than numerical identity.


This distinction transforms the economics of inference.


A smaller latent representation occupies less memory.


Less memory requires less bandwidth.


Reduced bandwidth allows higher throughput.


Higher throughput reduces serving cost.


Longer contexts become economically practical without requiring proportionally larger memory
systems.


One architectural decision propagates throughout the entire inference stack.


What makes MLA particularly elegant is that it attacks the problem at its true point of origin. Previous
optimizations largely accepted the existence of enormous KV caches and attempted to access them
more intelligently. MLA questions whether those caches should have existed in their original form at all.


This is a recurring pattern throughout the evolution of computing.


Engineers initially optimize an expensive operation.


Eventually someone asks whether the operation itself is necessary.


The latter question almost always proves more consequential.


Importantly, MLA should not be understood as an isolated breakthrough emerging in intellectual
isolation. It belongs to a broader movement within modern AI engineering toward representation
efficiency. Grouped Query Attention reduced redundancy among attention heads. Quantization reduced
numerical precision without significantly sacrificing model quality. FlashAttention minimized
unnecessary memory transfers. Compiler technologies increasingly fused kernels to eliminate
intermediate writes. Collectively, these developments reveal an industry slowly abandoning the
assumption that abundant computation justifies abundant movement.


The shift is philosophical as much as technical.


For years, language model research implicitly treated memory as passive storage—a warehouse
through which information simply passed on its way toward computation. Increasingly, memory has
become an active participant in architectural design. Representations are compressed, reorganized,
partitioned and scheduled with the same care previously reserved for neural network topology itself.


In hindsight, this evolution appears almost inevitable.


Once computation became inexpensive relative to communication, the next generation of innovation
was always going to occur within the pathways connecting them.


Yet memory represents only one form of communication.


The moment a language model expands beyond a single accelerator, another, even more formidable
challenge emerges. Information must now travel not merely across memory buses within one GPU but
across thousands of GPUs connected by networks that, despite extraordinary engineering, remain
painfully slow compared with the speed of computation occurring inside each device.


The Memory Wall, formidable though it is, turns out to be only the first wall.


Beyond it lies the network.
